summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the mother made an application seeking a variation to final parenting orders made 5 November 2025 to include an injunctive order pursuant to s 68B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) either by use of either the common law slip rule in the form of machinery or consequential orders or alternatively utilising the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) – Where application opposed by the father on the basis that it constitutes a de facto appeal or re-litigation – Neither course available to the Court – Orders made dismissing the application

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

McGuire J:

APPLICATIONS

This is an application by Ms Gerver (“the mother”) following final parenting orders made 5 November 2025 seeking a variation of those orders by use of either the common law “slip rule” in the form of “machinery” or “consequential” orders or alternatively utilising the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the rules”) and specifically rule 10.
The mother seeks the “variation” to include in the final orders an injunctive order pursuant to s 68B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) and as set out in her Response of 23 September 2024 as follows:
[5]. Pursuant to section 68B of the Family Law Act 1975, the father be restrained from contacting or approaching, or attempting to contact or approach, the Children [Mr D], [X] and [Y] by any means whatsoever, including through any third party.
The substantive parenting orders made and entered 5 November 2025 inter-alia provide that the relevant children spend no time with or have no communication with Mr Gerver (“the father”). That order was consistent with the position prosecuted by the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”).
The orders are silent as to the s 68B injunctive order sought by the mother.
In her counsel’s written Case Outline filed 23 September 2025, the final paragraph states thus:
S 68B Restraint:
[30]. The Court would find restraints on the father as proposed by the mother are necessary for the welfare of the children or the protection of the mother for the reasons expressed at [8] – [10] above.
Neither the father’s Case Outline nor the ICL specifically reference the s 68B order albeit that the orders sought by the ICL in her aide-memoir handed to the Court at final submission specifically sought the s 68B injunctive orders.
The transcript discloses, and properly conceded by counsel for the mother, that no specific submissions were made in respect of the injunctive orders where the primary issue at all times was a question of unacceptable risk posed by the father to the children. Nevertheless, it is clear that the Court was asked to make necessary inferences towards such an order in light of the cases put by the mother and the ICL.
Relevantly, no state court apprehended domestic violence order or similar was extant at the time of the trial with the most recent order being made in early 2020 in favour of the mother and expiring in early 2024.
Rule 10.13 relied on by the mother states:
10.13 Varying or setting aside orders
(1) The court may at any time vary or set aside an order, if:
(a) it was made in the absence of a party; or

(b) it was obtained by fraud; or

(c) it is interlocutory; or

(d) it is an injunction or for the appointment of a receiver; or

(e) it does not reflect the intention of the court; or

(f) the party in whose favour it was made consents; or

(g) there is a clerical mistake in the order; or
(h) there is an error arising in the order from an accidental slip or omission.
...

THE MOTHER’S CASE

The mother argues that her case for a s 68B injunctive order was clearly flagged in her Response and Case Outline.
The mother says that the findings of the Court at [113], [114] and [122], of the reasons for judgment clearly ground the making of the order and that there is an obvious accidental omission which should be rectified as being in the best interests of the children with reference to their safety.
Relevantly, the reasons for judgment disclose the following:
[113]. I am satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the mother continues to suffer fear and trepidation of the father himself and vicariously in respect of her children should they have a relationship with him. I find that the mother is of a vulnerable and timid personality and where her fears have been compounded by a power imbalance in the marriage whereby the father was coercive and controlling within the family unit. ...

[114]. Relevantly, I find the father’s propensity for coercive and controlling behaviour has continued post-separation ...

[122]. I find the mother to be timid and vulnerable of disposition and I accept her fears of the father both personally and for her children to be genuine and based on past experience and hence I am satisfied on the balance of probabilities that her parenting capacity is likely to be negatively impacted should there be any orders for time between the children and their father even should such time be supervised. ...

First, the mother argues that the inclusion of the s 68B order could be made utilising the “machinery or consequential” orders open to the Court with the assistance of the “slip rule” to give enforcement to the substantive order for no time or communication between the father and the children. The mother says that such use of the slip rule and the inclusion of the s 68B order gives holistic relevance to the orders made in light of the reasons set out above.
Alternatively, the mother argues that the Court may utilise rule 10.13(1)(d) or perhaps subrules (1)(e) and (1)(h).

THE FATHER’S CASE

The father opposes the orders sought. He argues that the “slip rule” is not circumstantially available to the Court in the sense argued by the mother and its utilisation would be contrary to settled authority and, in fact, constitute a de facto appeal or re-litigation.
Secondly, the father argues that the relevant rules relied upon by the mother have no application here.

THE ICL’S CASE

The ICL appeared at the hearing of this matter and argued only that the inclusion of the s 68B order would be in the best interests of the children.

CONSIDERATION

A close reading of rule 10.13 does not persuade me that the utilisation of this rule is available here. The rule relevantly states at (1) that the Court “may at any time vary or set aside an order”. There is no order here to be varied or set aside. Rather, the mother seeks the introduction of a new order.
Specifically, subsection (1)(e) is not available to the Court where it is not possible to assert the intention of the Court to specifically make the s 68B order.
Similarly, subsection (1)(g) is not available as the orders do not disclose a simple clerical mistake.
I am not persuaded that the “slip rule” is available to the Court to rectify the orders in the sense sought by the mother. It is clear that superior courts have inherent power to amend their own orders to give effect to the meaning of the judgment or the intention of the Court, but that power does not extend to the making of a further or discrete order.
As the Full Court noted in Milham & Stanford:[1]
[26]. It must also be borne in mind that for the purposes of the operation of the slip rule, an omission or mistake should not be treated as accidental if the proposed amendment requires the exercise of an independent discretion or as a matter upon which a real difference might exist.
(Footnotes omitted)
I am of the view that the proposed amendment sought by the mother requires an exercise of independent discretion where is clear that the father per se opposes such an order.
It is clear that the Court has a power to make what is termed “machinery or consequential” orders.[2]
Such a consequential order would follow logically or of necessity from an existing substantive order. It cannot be a discrete or novel order. It cannot be an order which varies the prior order. It cannot affect independently the substantive rights of either party.
The order sought here by the mother is, in my view, a discrete and independent order and not consequential upon any extant prior substantive order. There is no direct nexus to the relevant order asserting no time or communication for the children with the father. That order stands alone. The order sought by the mother is an injunctive order discrete in its wording and in the potential ramifications for a breach it is a standalone order as to intent and enforcement. Consequently, it cannot be seen to be a “machinery” or “consequential” order.

CONCLUSION

Consequently, I am of the view that the mother has recourse neither under the rules nor by reason of the slip rule/machinery or consequential orders and her recourse appears limited to appeal or re-litigation. The application will be dismissed.
